model_narrative: We route flow across a network of cities connected by physical links. For each destination city we decide how much of that destination's flow to send along each available directed arc between two cities. Sending flow along an arc costs its length times the flow carried. The goal is to choose all the arc flows so that the total routing cost across the whole network is as small as possible.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the cities in the network; this same set is also used as the alias ip and the alias ipp, so a triple over i, ip, ipp ranges over every ordered combination of three cities

params.name: uarc
params.doc: the undirected links of the network with their length, listed once per unordered pair of cities; a value is present only for pairs that are physically connected, in distance units

params.name: darc
params.doc: the directed-arc length between two cities, obtained by reading each undirected link in both directions; it is positive exactly when the two cities are directly connected and equals zero for every disconnected ordered pair, so a nonzero darc marks an available one-way arc and its value is the arc length in distance units

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the flow of the commodity destined for city given by the first index that travels along the directed arc from the second-index city to the third-index city; the first index labels the destination commodity and the last two indices identify the directed arc carrying it

vars.name: cost
vars.doc: the total routing cost over the whole network, in distance-weighted flow units

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: Every city other than a commodity's own destination must pass that destination's flow through with a little to spare. For each pairing of a destination with any city that is not that destination, the total of the destination's flow arriving at the city over the arcs that lead into it must be at least the total of the destination's flow leaving the city over the arcs that lead out of it, plus one more unit. Only arcs that actually exist between connected cities count on either side. When the city being balanced is the destination itself, no such requirement applies.

constraints.name: cost
constraints.intent: The total routing cost gathers the length-weighted flow over the entire network. For every destination commodity and every ordered pair of cities, multiply the flow of that commodity on the arc from the first city to the second by the length of that arc, and add it up across all commodities and all ordered city pairs. Set the total cost variable equal to this sum.

query: Salt Lake's terminal goes into renovation next quarter and operations needs headroom there. There's a proposal on the table to hold the combined volume arriving into Salt Lake — over its five inbound links from Chicago, Dallas, Kansas City, Los Angeles, and Portland, counting every city's traffic together — to at most 16 units. What does the optimizer return for total routing cost under that ceiling?
expected_pyomo: model.slc_inbound_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[i, n, 'salt-lake'] for i in model.i for n in ['chicago', 'dallas', 'kansas-cty', 'losangeles', 'portland']) <= 16)

query: The Boston-to-Chicago gateway keeps getting flagged for congestion. The network team is pitching a balance rule for Boston-origin traffic: of everything it sends out of Boston — the Chicago departures plus the Washington departures together — at most a quarter may leave on the Boston-to-Chicago leg. Plug that in and tell me the new total cost.

query: Washington is being groomed as our east-coast staging yard. We're looking at a service rule: every other city's commodity has to land at least 2 units into Washington, counting its flow over the three inbound links — from Boston, from Kansas City, and from Memphis. What's the resulting routing bill?

query: Two corridor projects would run at the same time next quarter: the Dallas–Memphis link drops to 6 units of combined two-way traffic counting all commodities, and Memphis–Kansas City drops to 4 on the same basis. We're evaluating both restrictions together as one package. What total cost does the optimizer settle on?
expected_pyomo:
model.dal_mem_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[i, 'dallas', 'memphis'] + model.x[i, 'memphis', 'dallas'] for i in model.i) <= 6)
model.mem_kc_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[i, 'memphis', 'kansas-cty'] + model.x[i, 'kansas-cty', 'memphis'] for i in model.i) <= 4)

query: Let's try the Washington consolidation idea the board floated: push at least 8 units of combined traffic — all commodities together — over the Washington-to-Kansas-City leg so the new sorting facility gets utilization. What's the new network cost?
expected_pyomo: model.wdc_kc_floor = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[i, 'wash-dc', 'kansas-cty'] for i in model.i) >= 8)

query: The Chicago–Portland link is the longest haul we own at 130, and the plan still puts traffic on it. Couldn't we just keep every commodity off that link, in both directions? What's the model seeing that I'm not?

query: Chicago's own traffic rides the pricey direct leg into Boston while the Washington relay sits unused for it. Shouldn't the Chicago commodity's flow on the direct Chicago-to-Boston leg be no more than what it brings into Boston off the Washington-to-Boston leg? What's keeping the model from leaning on the relay?

query: Every link 75 or longer — Chicago–Portland, Chicago–Salt Lake, Dallas–Los Angeles, and Dallas–Salt Lake — is a premium mountain corridor, and the plan piles 22 units onto them. Wouldn't capping the combined two-way volume across all four of those corridors, all commodities included, at 14 units force a saner pattern? What's the tradeoff I'm missing?
expected_pyomo: model.long_arc_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[i, a, b] + model.x[i, b, a] for i in model.i for (a, b) in [('chicago', 'portland'), ('chicago', 'salt-lake'), ('dallas', 'losangeles'), ('dallas', 'salt-lake')]) <= 14)

query: Every leg a unit rides is another handling charge, and Portland-origin freight gets touched 16 times across the network today. Shouldn't a leaner routing tree be able to move all of Portland's traffic with a total of at most 14 units carried across all the network's links combined? Walk me through what's pulling the plan the other way.
